Translation Table Definitions

Translation Table Definitions

Each definition in a translation table consist of a single line of up to 1024 characters arranged in three columns. Most definitions are mappings that contain an external definition, its equivalent Uniface definition, and an optional description. In device translation tables, definitions are grouped into sections. Both device and keyboard translation tables include a definition specifying the meta variant and the type of translation table.

Each line in a translation table can contain up to 1024 characters arranged in three columns, separated by one or more spaces or tabs.WIth the exception of device section delimiters and the meta_Variant definition, the columns contain the following:

  • Column one contains the external definition. This can be an output sequence used by the device to perform the requested action, or the character sequences sent by the keyboard or database. The sum of all output sequences in the device translation table must not exceed 9000 bytes.
  • Column two contains the Uniface definition, which can be a Uniface mnemonic for a function, or a character sequence. For example, Uniface mnemonics include such as ^store, ^bold, and ^blink. Character sequences contain one or more characters in the indicated meta variant, and can be represented by mnemonics, decimal Unicode.
  • Column three is optional and contains comments. Except for the mode_ definitions, comments are ignored by the compiler.

For example, the following line in a device translation table defines the escape sequence needed to clear the screen for a VT220-compatible terminal:

^^Output Sequence Uniface Function Comments
^esc[2J clear_screen clear full screen

The following line in a keyboard translation table, :

^^Character Sequence Uniface Function Comments
^esc^S ^STORE Store data in the database
Related Links
  • Device Translation Table Mnemonics

    Uniface uses a set of mnemonics to identify particular functions. When you define a device translation table, you define the device control codes that implement a particular function.

  • Meta Character Sets

    Meta character sets are used for translating installable character sets and defining translation tables. In earlier versions of Uniface, meta character sets were used as internal character sets instead of Unicode.

  • Uniface Mappings to Installable Character Sets

    During Uniface installation, it is possible to choose a character set that is appropriate for one or more languages. Thus Western European is appropriate for English, German, French and so on, and Cyrillic is appropriate for Slavic languages such as Russian.
